title: Confinement-Connectivity Coupling Enables High-Efficiency Piezoionic Transduction
authors: Tofayel Ahammad Ovee, Daniel Kroeger, Jean-François Louf
abstract: Piezoionic hydrogels offer a route to mechanically driven bioelectronic interfaces, but their output is limited by rapid, symmetric ion redistribution that dissipates charge gradients. In biological electrocytes, efficient signal generation arises from the coupling of ion selectivity with spatial confinement that regulates transport. Here, we introduce a confinement-connectivity design strategy for piezoionic hydrogels, implemented through a supramolecular poly(vinyl alcohol)-glycerol-cucurbit[5]uril (PVA-glycerol-CB[5]) mesoporous network with a layered Negative-Neutral-Positive architecture that simultaneously increases pore fraction while reducing characteristic pore size. This architecture constrains ionic redistribution while maintaining a large mobile-ion reservoir, enabling deformation-driven charge separation. Compression generates peak outputs of ~180 mV and ~9 mA and elicits synchronized electromyographic responses in the mouse sciatic nerve without external power. These results establish confinement-connectivity coupling, rather than bulk conductivity, as a materials design framework in which coupling pore connectivity and confinement governs piezoionic transduction.
summary: The paper presents a new way to design hydrogels for bioelectronics by using a specific pore structure to prevent ion dissipation. By controlling both connectivity and confinement, the researchers created a material that generates significant electrical power from mechanical movement, even enough to trigger nerve responses.
main_problem: Piezoionic hydrogels suffer from low output efficiency because rapid, symmetric ion redistribution dissipates the charge gradients needed for signal generation.
main_result: A confinement-connectivity design strategy using a mesoporous PVA-glycerol-CB[5] network enables high-efficiency piezoionic transduction, producing up to 180 mV and 9 mA and driving electromyographic responses in nerves.
method: The researchers implemented a layered Negative-Neutral-Positive architecture in a supramolecular mesoporous network to simultaneously increase pore fraction and reduce pore size.
model_or_system: A supramolecular poly(vinyl alcohol)-glycerol-cucurbit[5]uril (PVA-glycerol-CB[5]) mesoporous hydrogel with a layered architecture designed to regulate ionic transport.
key_observables: Peak voltage (~180 mV), peak current (~9 mA), and electromyographic responses in the mouse sciatic nerve.
important_parameters: Pore fraction, characteristic pore size, and the coupling of pore connectivity and confinement.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a design strategy for hydrogels, describes the implementation of the mesoporous network, demonstrates the resulting electrical output under compression, and validates the device via biological interface testing.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
